title: Earth's Internal Structure — Crust, Mantle, Core
ordering_justification: After cosmic origin (topics 1-6), the student must understand Earth's layered interior before studying plate tectonics, earthquakes, and volcanism. This topic is foundational for all geomorphic processes.
concept_checklist: Earth has 3 major layers: Crust (5-70 km), Mantle (2900 km thick), Core (3471 km radius).
Continental crust (Sial: Si+Al, density ~2.7) vs Oceanic crust (Sima: Si+Mg, density ~3.0).
Mohorovičić Discontinuity (Moho) separates crust from mantle; Gutenberg Discontinuity separates mantle from core.
P-waves travel through solids and liquids; S-waves only through solids — S-wave shadow zone proves liquid outer core.
Asthenosphere (upper mantle, 100-250 km) is partially molten; plates float and move on it.
Outer core is liquid iron-nickel (generates Earth's magnetic field via dynamo effect); Inner core is solid iron-nickel.
Temperature increases ~25-30°C per km near surface (geothermal gradient); core temperature ~5500°C.
Evidence: seismic waves, meteorite composition, Earth's density (5.5 g/cm³ vs surface rocks 2.7 g/cm³), magnetic field.

blocks.title: Start Here
blocks.text: You stand on rock that is only 0.5% of Earth's radius deep. Beneath your feet lies a 6,371 km journey to the centre — through rock hot enough to glow, metal liquid enough to flow, and iron so compressed it forms a crystal ball the size of the Moon. We cannot drill there, yet we know its structure precisely. How? Earthquakes tell us.
The Three-Layer Model

Earth's interior is divided into three concentric shells based on **chemical composition**: the **Crust** (outermost, thinnest), the **Mantle** (thickest, 84% of Earth's volume), and the **Core** (innermost, densest). This division was established through the study of **seismic waves** generated by earthquakes.

Think of a hard-boiled egg: the shell is the crust (thin, brittle), the white is the mantle (thick, semi-solid), and the yolk is the core (dense, metallic). The proportions are different, but the layered principle is the same.
The Crust

The crust is Earth's outermost solid layer. It comes in two types: **Continental crust** (thicker, 30-70 km, less dense, ~2.7 g/cm³, composed of **Sial** — silicon + aluminium, mainly granite) and **Oceanic crust** (thinner, 5-10 km, denser, ~3.0 g/cm³, composed of **Sima** — silicon + magnesium, mainly basalt).
The Mantle

Below the crust lies the **mantle**, extending to a depth of **2,900 km**. It constitutes about **84% of Earth's volume** and **67% of its mass**. The mantle is composed of **silicate rocks** rich in iron and magnesium (olivine, pyroxene). The upper mantle contains the **asthenosphere** (100-250 km depth) — a partially molten, plastic zone on which tectonic plates float and move.

blocks.title: Key Insight
blocks.text: The asthenosphere is NOT fully liquid — it is 1-5% molten, making it behave like a very thick fluid over geological timescales. This partial melting enables **convection currents** that drive plate tectonics.
The Core

The **core** extends from 2,900 km to the centre (6,371 km). It is divided into the **Outer Core** (2,900-5,150 km, **liquid** iron-nickel, temperature ~4,000-5,000°C) and the **Inner Core** (5,150-6,371 km, **solid** iron-nickel despite temperatures reaching ~5,500°C — kept solid by immense pressure of **360 GPa**).

blocks.title: Why Is the Inner Core Solid?
blocks.text: Although temperature exceeds the melting point of iron at surface pressure, the **extreme pressure** (3.6 million atmospheres) at the centre raises the melting point above the actual temperature. Pressure wins over temperature — hence solid.

terms.definition: Continental crust layer: **Si**licon + **Al**uminium. Less dense (~2.7 g/cm³).
Partially molten upper mantle (100-250 km). Plates float on it.
Rigid outer shell = crust + uppermost solid mantle (~100 km thick).
Rate of temperature increase with depth: ~25-30°C per km near surface.

content_sections.title: NCERT Reference — Interior of the Earth

blocks.text: Chapter 3 'Interior of the Earth' covers sources of information about Earth's interior, structure of the Earth, and earthquakes/volcanoes.
key_facts: Direct sources: mining, volcanic eruptions, drilling
Indirect sources: seismic waves, gravitational anomalies, magnetic field
Earth's layers based on chemical composition and physical properties

blocks.text: NCERT Key Points — Class 11, Chapter 3

blocks.title: Sources of Information (Direct)
items: Volcanic eruptions bring material from depth
Deep drilling projects (deepest: Kola Superdeep Borehole, Russia — 12.2 km)

blocks.title: Sources of Information (Indirect)
items: **Seismic waves** — most important source: velocity changes reveal density changes
**Gravitational anomalies** — variations in g indicate mass distribution
**Magnetic field** — indicates metallic core with convection

blocks.text: NCERT emphasises that **seismic waves are the most important source** of information about Earth's interior. The chapter explains how **P-waves** (Primary, longitudinal, travel through all media) and **S-waves** (Secondary, transverse, travel only through solids) create **shadow zones** that reveal the liquid outer core.

blocks.caption: P-wave and S-wave shadow zones revealing the liquid outer core (103°-143° for P-waves; 103°-180° for S-waves).

blocks.title: NCERT Exam Focus
blocks.text: UPSC frequently asks about: (1) Why S-waves cannot pass through outer core, (2) Difference between Moho and Gutenberg discontinuities, (3) Evidence for liquid outer core. All three are directly from NCERT Ch. 3.

NCERT also distinguishes between the **chemical classification** (Crust-Mantle-Core) and the **mechanical/physical classification** (Lithosphere-Asthenosphere-Mesosphere-Outer Core-Inner Core). UPSC has asked this distinction directly in Prelims.

content_sections.title: Advanced Analysis — Seismic Evidence and Deep Earth Dynamics

blocks.text: Seismic Waves as X-rays of the Earth

When an earthquake occurs, it generates **body waves** that travel through Earth's interior: **P-waves** (Primary/Push-pull, compressional, travel through solids AND liquids, speed 5-13 km/s) and **S-waves** (Secondary/Shake, shear, travel ONLY through solids, speed 3-7 km/s). The behaviour of these waves reveals internal boundaries.
Discontinuities — The Boundaries

Abrupt changes in seismic wave velocity mark boundaries between layers. These are called **discontinuities**: **Conrad Discontinuity** (between upper and lower continental crust, ~15 km), **Mohorovičić (Moho) Discontinuity** (crust-mantle boundary, discovered 1909, depth 5-70 km), **Repetti Discontinuity** (upper-lower mantle, ~700 km), **Gutenberg Discontinuity** (mantle-core boundary, 2,900 km — P-waves slow dramatically, S-waves disappear), **Lehmann Discontinuity** (outer-inner core boundary, 5,150 km — P-wave speed increases again, indicating solid inner core).

items.event: Mohorovičić discovers crust-mantle boundary using Croatian earthquake data
Gutenberg identifies mantle-core boundary at 2,900 km
Inge Lehmann discovers solid inner core (Lehmann Discontinuity)
Kola Superdeep Borehole drilling begins (reached 12.2 km by 1989)
Inner core rotation detected (slightly faster than mantle)

blocks.text: Earth's Magnetic Field and the Dynamo Effect

Earth's **magnetic field** is generated by convective motion of liquid iron in the **outer core** — the **geodynamo theory**. The rotation of Earth (Coriolis effect) organises these flows into patterns that sustain the dipole field. This field shields Earth from solar wind and has **reversed polarity** hundreds of times in geological history (recorded in ocean-floor basalts — key evidence for seafloor spreading).

blocks.title: Why This Matters for UPSC
blocks.text: Earth's magnetic field → paleomagnetism → evidence for seafloor spreading → plate tectonics. This is the chain UPSC tests. If you understand the dynamo, you understand why the magnetic field reverses, and that directly connects to the next topic (Plate Tectonics).
Chemical vs Mechanical Classification

blocks.title: Two Ways to Classify Earth's Interior
Advanced: How Do We Know Earth's Core Composition?
blocks.content: Three independent lines: (1) Earth's bulk density (5.5 g/cm³) vs surface rock (2.7) requires a dense core — only iron-nickel fits. (2) Iron meteorites (from differentiated asteroids) provide direct samples of planetary core material. (3) Seismic wave velocities through the core match laboratory measurements of iron at extreme pressure/temperature.

content_sections.title: Current Affairs Connections — Interior of the Earth

blocks.text: Recent Developments (2024-2026)

blocks.title: Inner Core Rotation Slowdown (2023-2024)
blocks.text: Research published in **Nature Geoscience (2023)** confirmed that Earth's inner core has **slowed its rotation** relative to the mantle since ~2009 and may have briefly reversed direction. This affects day length by fractions of a millisecond and has implications for long-term magnetic field evolution. UPSC Prelims could frame this as a statement-based question on inner core dynamics.

blocks.title: Ultra-Low Velocity Zones (ULVZs) at Core-Mantle Boundary
blocks.text: 2024-2025 studies using seismic tomography revealed extensive **ultra-low velocity zones** (areas where seismic waves slow by 20-50%) at the core-mantle boundary beneath the Pacific. These may be remnants of ancient tectonic plates that sank, or indicate partial melting. Relevant for Mains questions on deep-Earth dynamics.

blocks.title: India's Deep Drilling Ambitions (2025)
blocks.text: India's **Ministry of Earth Sciences** announced plans for a **scientific deep drilling program** in the Koyna region (Maharashtra) to study the relationship between reservoir-induced seismicity and crustal structure. This connects to understanding Indian plate stress patterns and earthquake prediction.

blocks.title: Chandrayaan-3 and Comparative Planetology (2023)
blocks.text: **Chandrayaan-3's** success at the lunar south pole provides data about the Moon's internal structure through seismic experiments. Comparing lunar interior (small iron core, no dynamo) with Earth's (large liquid outer core, active dynamo) is a common UPSC framing for science questions.

content_sections.title: Examiner Traps — Interior of the Earth

blocks.text: Common Misconceptions UPSC Tests

blocks.correct: The mantle is mostly SOLID rock; only the asthenosphere (1-5%) is partially molten. It behaves plastically over millions of years but is rigid for short-duration events like earthquakes.

blocks.wrong: S-waves are completely absent beyond the outer core
blocks.correct: S-waves DO reappear in the inner core (because it is SOLID). They are absent only in the LIQUID outer core (103°-180° shadow zone). Lehmann discovered this in 1936.

blocks.wrong: The inner core is liquid because temperature is highest there
blocks.correct: The inner core is SOLID despite ~5,500°C because pressure (360 GPa) raises the melting point above the actual temperature. Pressure > Temperature → solid.

blocks.wrong: Moho discontinuity is the boundary between mantle and core
blocks.correct: Moho separates CRUST from MANTLE. The mantle-core boundary is the GUTENBERG discontinuity at 2,900 km depth.

blocks.wrong: Earth's magnetic field is produced by a solid iron magnet in the core
blocks.correct: It is produced by CONVECTION of LIQUID iron in the outer core (geodynamo effect). A solid iron magnet would lose magnetism above its Curie temperature (~770°C), and the core is far hotter.

blocks.wrong: Continental crust is thinner than oceanic crust because continents erode
blocks.correct: Continental crust is THICKER (30-70 km) but LESS DENSE. Oceanic crust is THINNER (5-10 km) but DENSER. Thickness and density are inversely related here.

blocks.wrong: The deepest humans have drilled reaches the mantle
blocks.correct: The deepest drill (Kola Superdeep Borehole, 12.2 km) barely scratched the upper crust. The Moho is typically 30-70 km under continents. We have NEVER directly sampled the mantle in situ.

blocks.text: MOHO = Mantle Onset Here, Okay? | GUTENBERG = Gets Underneath To Enter Nickel-Bearing Earth's Reservoir (of liquid iron) | LEHMANN = Liquid Ends Here, Manifests A Nickel Nugget

pyqs.question_text:
Consider the following statements:
1. The Earth's inner core is in liquid state.
2. The Earth's outer core is in solid state.
3. The Gutenberg discontinuity lies between the mantle and the core.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
pyqs.explanation: Only statement 3 is correct. The inner core is SOLID (despite extreme heat, pressure keeps it solid). The outer core is LIQUID (enabling the geodynamo). Gutenberg discontinuity (2,900 km) does separate mantle from core.

pyqs.question_text: The__(1)__ discontinuity separates the crust from the mantle, while the __(2)__ discontinuity separates the mantle from the core. Fill the blanks.
pyqs.explanation: Moho (discovered 1909) is at 5-70 km depth. Gutenberg (discovered 1914) is at 2,900 km.

pyqs.question_text: Discuss how the study of seismic waves has helped in understanding the interior structure of the Earth.
pyqs.answer_text: Seismic waves (P and S) provide the most important indirect evidence about Earth's interior. P-waves travel through all media but slow at the Gutenberg discontinuity (2900 km); S-waves cannot pass through the liquid outer core creating a shadow zone from 103°-180°. This proves the outer core is liquid. The reappearance of P-waves (with increased speed) and converted S-waves beyond 5150 km indicates the inner core is solid (Lehmann 1936). Wave velocity changes at Moho (crustal base), 400 km and 670 km (mantle transition zones) reveal layered structure.
pyqs.explanation: Key Mains answer structure: define P/S waves → explain shadow zones → deduce liquid/solid layers → mention discontinuities → Indian context (seismic zones).

mcq_questions.question_text:
Consider the following statements regarding Earth's internal structure:
1. The continental crust is composed mainly of Sial (Silicon + Aluminium).
2. The oceanic crust is thicker but less dense than continental crust.
3. The Mohorovičić discontinuity marks the boundary between upper and lower crust.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
mcq_questions.explanation: Statement 1 is correct — continental crust is Sial. Statement 2 is wrong — oceanic crust is THINNER (5-10 km) but DENSER (3.0 g/cm³). Statement 3 is wrong — Moho separates crust from MANTLE, not upper from lower crust (that's Conrad discontinuity).

mcq_questions.question_text:
Consider the following statements:
1. P-waves can travel through both solids and liquids.
2. S-waves can travel only through solids.
3. Both P-waves and S-waves are surface waves.
4. The shadow zone for S-waves extends from 103° to 180°.
How many of the above statements are correct?
mcq_questions.explanation: Statements 1, 2, and 4 are correct. Statement 3 is wrong — P-waves and S-waves are BODY waves (travel through interior), not surface waves. Surface waves (Love and Rayleigh) travel along the surface.

mcq_questions.question_text:
Assertion (A): The inner core of Earth is solid despite temperatures exceeding 5000°C.
Reason (R): The immense pressure at Earth's centre raises the melting point of iron above the actual temperature.

options.label: A
options.text: Both A and R are correct, and R is the correct explanation of A

options.label: B
options.text: Both A and R are correct, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A

options.label: C
options.text: A is correct but R is incorrect

options.label: D
options.text: A is incorrect but R is correct

mcq_questions.explanation: The inner core IS solid (~5500°C) and the reason IS that pressure (~360 GPa, 3.6 million atmospheres) raises iron's melting point above the actual temperature. This is the correct mechanism — R fully explains A.

mcq_questions.question_text:
Assertion (A): Earth's magnetic field is generated by convection currents in the liquid outer core.
Reason (R): The outer core is composed of solid iron which acts as a permanent magnet.

options.label: A
options.text: Both A and R are correct, and R is the correct explanation of A

options.label: B
options.text: Both A and R are correct, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A

options.label: C
options.text: A is correct but R is incorrect

options.label: D
options.text: A is incorrect but R is correct

mcq_questions.explanation: A is correct — the geodynamo theory explains magnetic field generation through convection in LIQUID iron of the outer core. R is incorrect — the outer core is LIQUID (not solid), and even if it were solid, iron loses its permanent magnetism above Curie temperature (~770°C).

mcq_questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT a correct characteristic of the asthenosphere?

options.label: A
options.text: It lies in the upper mantle between 100-250 km depth

options.label: B
options.text: It is completely molten and behaves like a liquid

options.label: C
options.text: Tectonic plates float and move on it

options.label: D
options.text: It is partially molten (1-5%) and behaves plastically

mcq_questions.explanation: Option B is INCORRECT (and therefore the answer) — the asthenosphere is NOT completely molten. It is only 1-5% partially molten, which makes it behave plastically over geological timescales. A, C, and D are all correct descriptions.

mcq_questions.question_text: Which of the following discontinuities is NOT correctly matched with its position?

options.label: A
options.text: Conrad — between upper and lower continental crust

options.label: C
options.text: Gutenberg — between upper and lower mantle

options.label: D
options.text: Lehmann — between outer and inner core

mcq_questions.explanation: C is incorrect — Gutenberg discontinuity is at the MANTLE-CORE boundary (2,900 km), not between upper and lower mantle. The boundary between upper and lower mantle is the Repetti discontinuity (~700 km). A, B, D are correctly matched.

mcq_questions.question_text:
A major earthquake occurs in the Pacific Ocean. A seismograph station at 120° angular distance from the epicentre records P-waves but NOT S-waves. What can be inferred?

Context: The station is within the S-wave shadow zone (103°-180°).

options.label: A
options.text: The earthquake was too weak to generate S-waves

options.label: B
options.text: A liquid layer exists between the earthquake source and the station, blocking S-waves

options.label: D
options.text: S-waves always arrive later and have not reached yet

mcq_questions.explanation: At 120° from the epicentre, the station lies within the S-wave shadow zone (103°-180°). S-waves cannot pass through liquids. This proves that a liquid layer (the outer core) blocks the S-wave path. This was the key evidence for the liquid outer core.

mcq_questions.question_text:
A recent research paper reports that Earth's inner core rotation has slowed relative to the mantle.

In light of the above passage, consider the following:
1. This could affect Earth's day length by fractions of a millisecond.
2. The inner core rotates independently because it floats in the liquid outer core.
3. Changes in inner core rotation have no effect on Earth's magnetic field.
Which of the above inferences can be drawn?
mcq_questions.explanation: Inference 1 is valid — angular momentum transfer between core and mantle can affect day length (confirmed by 2023 Nature Geoscience study). Inference 2 is valid — the inner core is mechanically decoupled from the mantle by the liquid outer core, enabling independent rotation. Inference 3 is invalid — core dynamics directly influence the geodynamo and therefore the magnetic field.

mcq_questions.question_text:
Match List-I (Discontinuity) with List-II (Discoverer and Year):

List-I: (a) Moho, (b) Gutenberg, (c) Lehmann, (d) Conrad
List-II: (1) Inge Lehmann, 1936, (2) Andrija Mohorovičić, 1909, (3) Beno Gutenberg, 1914, (4) Victor Conrad, 1925
mcq_questions.explanation: Moho — Mohorovičić (1909), Gutenberg — Gutenberg (1914), Lehmann — Lehmann (1936), Conrad — Conrad (1925). The discontinuities are named after their discoverers.

mcq_questions.question_text:
Match List-I (Layer) with List-II (Characteristic):

List-I: (a) Lithosphere, (b) Asthenosphere, (c) Outer Core, (d) Inner Core
List-II: (1) Solid iron-nickel, (2) Liquid iron-nickel, (3) Partially molten, plastic, (4) Rigid, includes crust and upper mantle
mcq_questions.explanation: Lithosphere is rigid (crust + upper mantle). Asthenosphere is partially molten and plastic. Outer core is liquid iron-nickel (generates magnetic field). Inner core is solid iron-nickel (kept solid by pressure).

mcq_questions.question_text:
Consider the following statements about Earth's crust:
1. Oceanic crust is older than continental crust on average.
2. Continental crust has higher silica content than oceanic crust.
3. The thickness of continental crust is maximum under young fold mountains.
4. Oceanic crust is continuously created at mid-ocean ridges and destroyed at subduction zones.
How many of the above statements are correct?
mcq_questions.explanation: Statement 1 is WRONG — oceanic crust is YOUNGER (< 200 Myr, continuously recycled) while continental crust can be up to 4 Byr old. Statements 2, 3, and 4 are correct. Continental crust is granitic (more silica); it's thickest under Himalayas (~70 km); oceanic crust is created/destroyed at plate boundaries.

mcq_questions.question_text: The average density of Earth is 5.5 g/cm³, while the density of surface rocks averages only 2.7 g/cm³. What does this discrepancy indicate?

options.label: A
options.text: Earth's interior must contain much denser material to produce the observed average density

options.label: B
options.text: Surface rocks are not representative of Earth as they come from meteorites

options.label: C
options.text: Earth's mass has been incorrectly calculated

options.label: D
options.text: Surface rocks are lighter because of erosion processes

mcq_questions.explanation: If surface rocks are 2.7 g/cm³ but average density is 5.5, the interior must be far denser — this requires a heavy metallic core (iron-nickel, density ~13 g/cm³). This is one of the key lines of evidence for Earth's iron core.

mcq_questions.question_text:
Consider the following statements:
1. The geothermal gradient is uniform throughout Earth's interior at ~30°C per km.
2. The Kola Superdeep Borehole is the deepest artificial point on Earth.
3. Volcanic eruptions provide direct samples of upper mantle material.
Which of the above is/are correct?
mcq_questions.explanation: Statement 1 is wrong — the geothermal gradient (~25-30°C/km) applies only near the surface; it decreases with depth (otherwise the core would be impossibly hot). Statement 2 is correct (Kola, 12.2 km, Russia). Statement 3 is correct — xenoliths brought up by volcanic eruptions are actual mantle samples.

mcq_questions.question_text:
Consider the following statements:
1. Earth's mantle constitutes about 84% of Earth's volume.
2. The upper mantle contains olivine and pyroxene as dominant minerals.
3. Convection currents in the mantle are responsible for driving plate movements.
Which of the above is/are correct?
mcq_questions.explanation: All three are correct. The mantle is 84% of volume (2900 km thick shell). Upper mantle is dominated by olivine and pyroxene (ultramafic composition). Convection currents in the mantle (driven by internal heat) are the primary driving force for plate tectonics.

mcq_questions.question_text: Which one of the following is the most important source of information about the interior of the Earth?
mcq_questions.explanation: Seismic waves are the MOST important indirect source (as stated by NCERT). They reveal layer boundaries, physical states (solid/liquid), and density variations throughout Earth's entire interior. Volcanic eruptions and drilling provide only shallow/surface information. Gravity helps but doesn't reveal the full picture.
